WhenGisarealsemisimplegroup,thereisasurprisinginterplay betweenitsrepresentationtheoryandthatofitsmotiongroupG0,knownas theMackeyanalogy. Thepresentpaperextendsthisanalogytotheframework of q-deformations, for G = SL(2,R). In fact, we construct a deformation of SL(2,R)parametrizedby(q,t)∈R∗×R,whereqisthequantizationparameter + andtistheMackeyparameter. Weshowhowtherepresentationtheoryvaries along this deformation and we prove an analogue of the Connes-Kasparov isomorphismfortheq-deformedreducedgroupC*-algebra. Assume that G is a semisimple Lie group and K ⊂ G is a maximal compact subgroup. ThemotiongroupofGisthesemidirectproductG =K⋉(g/k),where 0 g/k is the quotient of the Lie algebra of G by the Lie algebra of K, viewed as an abelianLiegroupandequippedwiththeadjointactionofK. Onthebasisofideas of Mackey [25], Higson exhibited striking similarities between the representation theories of G and G in the complex case [19]. This analogy, named after Mackey, 0 was later carried out by Afgoustidis for real groups [1–3]. One of its main features is the existence of a natural bijection mapping continuously (see [4]) the tempered (or reduced unitary) dual of G to the unitary dual of G which preserves minimal 0 K-types. The Mackey analogy also led to a new proof of the Connes-Kasparov isomorphism: the contraction of G to G gives rise to a continuous field of reduced 0 group C*-algebras, called the Mackey deformation, which has constant K-theory. More recently, the authors of [9] constructed an embedding C∗(G )(cid:44)→C∗(G) and 0 r used it to characterize the Mackey bijection and the Connes-Kasparov map (with a prior approach [20] in the complex case). Inacompletelydifferentdirection, anotherinterestingdeformationofGisgiven by quantization. In a recent paper [11], De Commer constructed q-deformations of various convolution algebras of G related to a real Poisson-Lie structure on the complexificationofG. Ifq ̸=1isarealpositiveparameter,thereareq-analoguesof the enveloping ∗-algebra of g, of the Hecke algebra associated to (g,K) and of the maximal group C*-algebra of G which we respectively denote by U (g), R (g,K) q q and C∗(G). So far, the thorough study of these algebras and their representa- q tions has only concerned complex groups and SL(2,R). The irreducible ∗-representationsofC∗(G)andthenon-degeneratesimpleR (g,K)-modules,which q q are the analogues of the (g,K)-modules, are classified in [13] and [18] respectively. The authors of [14] constructed an analogue of the regular representation leading to a natural analogue of the reduced group C*-algebra C∗ (G). The spectrum of q,r the latter is characterized in [12]. The goal of the present paper is to unify these q-deformations with the Mackey deformation for G=SL(2,R), allowing for an extension of the Mackey analogy to q-deformed SL(2,R). Our work is inspired by what has been done by Monk and Voigt for complex semisimple groups [26]. Our analogue of the motion group for q-deformed SL(2,R) does not depend on q, it is the groupoid G⋆ of the action of K = SO(2) on the 2-sphere K\U, 0 where U =SU(2). In what follows, we construct a two-parameter continuous field ofC*-algebrasC∗(G)definedoverR∗ ×R,whoserestrictionto{1}×Risprecisely r + the Mackey deformation field and whose fibers, for each q ̸=1, are given by (cid:40) C∗ (G) if t̸=0, C∗(G) = qt,r r q,t C∗(G⋆) if t=0. 0 As we will see, this continuous field of C*-algebras is a reflection of a much more rigid field involving the corresponding (quantized) enveloping algebras. Moreover, for every fixed q ̸= 1, we prove that the restriction of C∗(G) to r {q}×[0,1] is isomorphic to the mapping cone field of a certain embedding αq :C∗(G⋆)(cid:44)→C∗ (G). 0 0 q,r We also show that this embedding induces a bijection between the spectrum of C∗(G⋆) and that of C∗ (G), as well as an isomorphism in K-theory. The latter 0 q,r resultmeansthatC∗(G) hasconstantK-theoryandthuscanbeinterpreted r |{q}×[0,1] as an analogue of the Connes-Kasparov isomorphism. Thepaperisorganizedasfollows. Inthefirstsection,weintroduceordevelopthe operator algebraic tools needed for our purpose. In the second section, we present the various convolution algebras involved in the two-parameter deformation. The third section is devoted to the construction of the continuous field of C*-algebras C∗(G). Inthelastsection,weconstructtheembeddingαq andestablishtheabove r 0 listed properties. Fortherestofthepaper,wewriteG=SL(2,R),K =SO(2),U =SU(2),unless it is explicitly stated otherwise. We denote by g, k, u the associated Lie algebras. Moreover, for any real Lie algebra a, the associated enveloping ∗-algebra U(a) is defined as the complexification of its universal enveloping algebra, endowed the unique ∗-structure such that the elements of a are skew-adjoint. 1. Operator algebraic preliminaries The goal of this first section is to introduce some background material that will facilitate the construction of the quantum Mackey deformation field. For the rest of the paper, the space of bounded operators and the space of compact operators over any Hilbert space H are denoted by B(H) and K(H), respectively.

We begin by recalling some aspects of the representation theory of G. Since this is discussed in many textbooks, for example [28,30], we will essentially focus on fixing notation for future use. TheHeckealgebraofthepair(g,K),definedin[23,§I.3],isdenotedbyR(g,K). It is the convolution algebra of K-finite distributions on G supported in K. As such, it is naturally endowed with a ∗-structure. The category of non-degenerate (or approximately unital) R(g,K)-modules corresponds to the category of (g,K)- modules,see[23,Theorem1.117]. Accordingly,a(g,K)-moduleissaidtobeunitary or unitarizable if the associated R(g,K)-module is. The unitary representation theory of G essentially reduces to the study of the (g,K)-modules. As an illustration of this principle, there is a one-one correspon- dence between irreducible unitary representations of G and unitarizable simple (g,K)-modules. It is defined as follows: if V is a simple unitary (g,K)-module then its Hilbert completion V¯ is an irreducible representation of G and V corre- sponds to the space K-finite vectors of V¯, see [6, §3].
